---
title: "Linear Models & Least Squares"
subtitle: "INFO 521 · Module 1 · Lecture A — Setup, Data, and the Linear Model"
author: "Greg Chism"
institute: "School of Information · University of Arizona"
date: last-modified
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Deck setup. Runs from the project root (execute-dir: project), so the shared
# helpers import the same way from any module depth.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "shared")

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import slide_helpers as sh

sh.use_slide_style()

# Load once; shared across this deck's figure cells (jupyter kernel state).
ds   = sh.load_nhanes()
age  = sh.primary_predictor(ds)                 # 'age' (years), the Project-1 predictor
sbp  = ds["y"]                                   # systolic BP (mmHg), the target
age_mean, age_sd = float(age.mean()), float(age.std())
age_z = (age - age_mean) / age_sd                # standardized age

# Scalar least-squares fit of SBP on standardized age (used on slides 8/12/13/14).
X    = np.column_stack([np.ones_like(age_z), age_z])
w    = np.linalg.solve(X.T @ X, X.T @ sbp)       # ŵ = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy
w0, w1 = float(w[0]), float(w[1])
w1_per_year = w1 / age_sd                         # convert slope to mmHg per year

# The week01 activity embed is a background-iframe slide attribute (literal URL —
# Quarto does not expand variables inside slide attributes; see CLAUDE.md).
```

## Learning outcomes {.smaller}

By the end of Lecture A you should be able to:

1. **Frame supervised regression** in PML notation — inputs, target, and what we
   are learning.
2. **Load and preprocess** the NHANES data and **state the leakage rule** for
   choosing features.
3. **Define** the linear model $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}$ and the
   mean-squared-error loss, and **compute $\mathcal{L}$ by hand**.
4. **Fit and interpret** a scalar least-squares line in clinical units.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: four outcomes = the arc of this lecture. The closed-form solution
and geometry are Lecture B; today is model + loss + data.
:::

## The course spine {.smaller}

One clinical outcome — **systolic blood pressure** — seen through **four lenses**
this term:

- **Least squares** (Module 1) — fit a line by minimizing squared error.
- **Maximum likelihood** (Module 3) — the same fit, now from a noise model.
- **Bayesian** (later) — carry uncertainty, not just a point estimate.
- **Discovery** (later) — structure and labels we did *not* hand the model.

[NHANES recurs in every module so the *methods* change, not the data — you build
intuition on one clinical problem.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Clay 01, trimmed). The point of one dataset all term: differences
you see across modules are about the *method*, not a new domain each week.
:::

## Supervised regression {.smaller}

Three kinds of machine learning:

::: {.fragment}
- **Supervised** — learn from **labeled** examples $(\mathbf{x}_n, y_n)$.
  *Regression* = continuous target; *classification* = discrete labels.
:::

::: {.fragment}
- **Unsupervised** — find structure with **no** labels (clustering, PCA) — the
  term's back half.
:::

::: {.fragment}
- **Reinforcement** — learn from a **reward** signal; out of scope here.
:::

::: {.fragment}
[**Regression is supervised learning with a continuous target.**]{style="color: var(--accent)"}
Given $(\mathbf{x}_n, y_n)$, learn $f$ so $\hat{y}=f(\mathbf{x})\approx y$ on new
$\mathbf{x}$ — here, systolic BP in mmHg.
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Clay 02). The three-way split orients students; we live in the
top-left box all of Module 1. Supervised = answers $y_n$ at training time;
regression = continuous target.
:::

## Notation & data structures {.smaller}

One example is a vector $\mathbf{x}_n \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$ → one prediction
$\hat{y}_n = \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}_n$. Stack the dataset:

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{—}\ \mathbf{x}_1^{\!\top}\ \text{—} \\ \vdots \\ \text{—}\ \mathbf{x}_N^{\!\top}\ \text{—} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times D},
\qquad
\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ \vdots \\ y_N \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}.
$$

Rows of the **design matrix** $\mathbf{X}$ are patients; $\mathbf{y}$ is the
**target vector**. (We write targets $y$ — not $t$.)

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: this notation is the spine for the whole term — N rows = patients,
D columns = features. Bishop's $t$ is our $y$.
:::

## The data — NHANES 2021–2022 {.smaller}

- **$N \approx 5{,}102$** U.S. adults; **target** = systolic blood pressure (mmHg).
- **Six leakage-safe features** (the Project-1 design):

| feature | meaning |
|---|---|
| `age` | age (years) — the Project-1 primary predictor |
| `bmi` | body-mass index |
| `waist` | waist circumference |
| `chol` | total cholesterol |
| `hdl` | HDL cholesterol |
| `hba1c` | glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) |

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: feature names are the loader's contract — `ds['features']` — not
invented. Diastolic BP is deliberately held out (next slide).
:::

## The leakage rule {.smaller}

**Exclude any predictor that is itself a blood-pressure measurement** — or that
would trivially determine the label.

- Diastolic BP (`dbp`) is **reserved**, never a feature: it co-measures the same
  physiology as the target.
- This is a **by-design** modeling choice, made *before* fitting — not something
  the algorithm discovers.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: leakage = letting the answer sneak into the inputs. This exact
choice returns in Project 2's cluster-vs-label reveal — flag it now.
:::

## A first look at the data

```{python}
#| label: fig-data-peek
#| fig-alt: "Two panels. Left: a histogram of systolic blood pressure for about 5100 NHANES adults, right-skewed and centered near 120 mmHg. Right: a scatter of standardized age versus systolic BP, showing a weak upward trend."
#| fig-cap: "NHANES systolic BP: marginal distribution (left) and vs. standardized age (right)."

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.6, 4.0))
axL.hist(sbp, bins=40, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["blue"], alpha=0.85)
axL.set_xlabel("systolic BP (mmHg)")
axL.set_ylabel("count")
axL.set_title("distribution of the target")

axR.scatter(age_z, sbp, s=10, alpha=0.18, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["blue"])
axR.set_xlabel("standardized age (z)")
axR.set_ylabel("systolic BP (mmHg)")
axR.set_title("target vs. a predictor")
sh.finalize(fig, alt="Histogram of systolic BP and a scatter of standardized age versus systolic BP.")
plt.show()
```

## The linear model {.smaller}

Predict the target as a weighted sum of the features (with an intercept):

$$
\hat{y} \;=\; \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x} \;=\; w_0 + w_1 x_1 + \cdots + w_D x_D .
$$

*(We absorb the intercept by appending a constant-1 feature, so
$\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{D+1}$.)*

- $w_0$ — the **intercept** (prediction when all features are 0).
- $w_j$ — the **per-unit effect** of feature $j$: how $\hat{y}$ changes when $x_j$
  rises by one unit, holding the rest fixed.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Clay 02/03). The intercept rides along as a constant "1" feature
in $\mathbf{x}$, so $\mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}$ covers it.
:::

## The loss: mean squared error {.smaller}

Measure fit by the average squared miss:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\bigl(y_n - \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}_n\bigr)^2 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{N}\,\lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}\rVert_2^2 .
$$

Why **squared** error? It is **smooth** (differentiable — we'll set its gradient
to zero), and it **penalizes large misses** disproportionately.

[A squared loss is also exactly what a Gaussian-noise model gives you — that tie
is Module 3.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: smooth + convex (Lecture B) is what makes the closed form possible.
The Gaussian connection (slide accent) is the M3 hook — plant it.
:::

## Worked: compute $\mathcal{L}$ by hand {.smaller}

Toy data $(x_n, y_n)$: $(0,1),\ (1,1),\ (2,3)$, with a guess $\hat{y}=0.5 + x$.

::: {.fragment}
**Predictions** $\hat{y}_n$: $\;0.5,\ 1.5,\ 2.5$.
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Residuals** $y_n-\hat{y}_n$: $\;0.5,\ -0.5,\ 0.5$.
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Square & average:**
$\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{3}\bigl(0.5^2 + (-0.5)^2 + 0.5^2\bigr) = \tfrac{1}{3}(0.75) = 0.25.$
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: walk each fragment. This is the mechanic the closed form will
minimize in Lecture B — and a checkpoint-style hand computation.
:::

## A scalar least-squares fit

```{python}
#| label: fig-scalar-fit
#| fig-alt: "Scatter of standardized age versus systolic blood pressure for NHANES adults in blue, with an upward-sloping vermillion least-squares fit line."
#| fig-cap: "Least-squares fit of systolic BP on standardized age."

grid = np.linspace(age_z.min(), age_z.max(), 200)
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.scatter(age_z, sbp, s=10, alpha=0.18, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["blue"], label="NHANES adults")
ax.plot(grid, w0 + w1 * grid, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["vermillion"], linestyle="-",
        label=fr"$\hat y = {w0:.1f} + {w1:.2f}\,z$")
ax.set_xlabel("standardized age (z)")
ax.set_ylabel("systolic BP (mmHg)")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", frameon=False)
sh.finalize(fig, alt="Least-squares fit line of systolic BP on standardized age over the NHANES scatter.")
plt.show()
```

## Interpreting the fit {.smaller}

The fitted line is $\hat{y} = \hat{w}_0 + \hat{w}_1 z$ with $\hat{w}_0 \approx 121.5$,
$\hat{w}_1 \approx 6.5$ (mmHg per **standard deviation** of age).

- **Intercept** $\hat{w}_0$: predicted SBP at *average* age (where $z=0$) — about
  **121.5 mmHg**, a clinically sensible central value.
- **Slope** $\hat{w}_1$: $+1$ SD of age ($\approx 17$ years) adds $\approx 6.5$ mmHg, i.e.
  **$\approx 0.38$ mmHg per year**.
- **Sanity check** — sign is positive (BP rises with age) and the magnitude is
  modest and plausible. Always translate weights back to units.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: the standardize-then-interpret two-step. Students must report slope
in clinical units (per year), not per-SD, when communicating.
:::

## Live demo: the least-squares explorer {background-iframe="https://gchism94.github.io/info521-activities-2026/week01-least-squares/" background-interactive="true"}

Drag the line, watch the loss respond — the tool is live on this slide.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: full-bleed interactive embed of the week01 LS explorer (PRIMARY
pattern, CLAUDE.md). The static fallback on the next slide is what prints /
exports to PDF.
:::

## Explorer — static fallback {.smaller}

*The iframe above does not print or export to PDF; this snapshot stands in.*

```{python}
#| label: fig-fallback
#| fig-alt: "Static snapshot standing in for the interactive explorer: the same standardized-age-versus-systolic-BP scatter with the least-squares fit line, captioned as a fallback for print and PDF."
#| fig-cap: "Static fallback snapshot of the week01 least-squares explorer."

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.scatter(age_z, sbp, s=10, alpha=0.18, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["blue"], label="NHANES adults")
ax.plot(grid, w0 + w1 * grid, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["vermillion"], label="least-squares fit")
ax.set_xlabel("standardized age (z)")
ax.set_ylabel("systolic BP (mmHg)")
ax.set_title("week01 explorer snapshot")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", frameon=False)
sh.finalize(fig, alt="Static fallback snapshot of the least-squares explorer.")
plt.show()
```

## Recap & bridge {.smaller}

We now have the two ingredients of least squares:

- a **model** — $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}$, and
- a **loss** — $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}) = \tfrac{1}{N}\lVert\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}\rVert_2^2$.

[**Next (Lecture B):** solve for the best $\mathbf{w}$ in closed form — set the
gradient to zero — and see the geometry behind it.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: model + loss → optimization. Lecture B derives ŵ=(XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy and
shows it as an orthogonal projection.
:::
